output.raw_output:
{
  "title": "Landlord Withholding Your Security Deposit? Here's How to Get It Back Fast",
  "meta_description": "Is your landlord withholding your security deposit? Learn your tenant rights, what landlords can deduct, and how to demand your deposit back in days.",
  "content_outline": [
    "Why Your Landlord Is Withholding Your Security Deposit (And Why They Might Be Wrong)",
    "Tenant Rights: How Long a Landlord Has to Return Your Deposit",
    "What a Landlord Can (and Cannot) Deduct From Your Security Deposit",
    "What to Do When Your Landlord Doesn't Return Your Deposit: Step-by-Step",
    "How a Security Deposit Dispute Letter Puts Pressure on Your Landlord"
  ],
  "key_points": [
    "Landlords must return deposits within state-specific timelines (usually 14-60 days) or face penalties",
    "Normal wear and tear cannot be deducted; only actual damage beyond ordinary use is a valid charge",
    "A formal demand letter is often the fastest way to recover withheld funds before escalating to small claims court",
    "DepositBack generates state-compliant security deposit demand letters in minutes, turning tenant frustration into legal leverage"
  ],
  "cta": "Don't let your landlord keep what isn't theirs. Generate your state-compliant security deposit demand letter with DepositBack today and recover your money fast.",
  "target_audience": "Frustrated US renters whose landlords are withholding, delaying, or wrongfully deducting from their security deposits",
  "word_count_target": 800,
  "email_sequence": [
    {
      "email_number": 1,
      "subject_line": "Your landlord can't just keep your deposit (here's why)",
      "preview_text": "Most withheld deposits are returned when tenants push back. Know your rights.",
      "body_copy": "If you're staring at an empty bank account wondering why your landlord is not returning your deposit, you're not alone—and you're not powerless.

Most tenants don't realize that a landlord withholding a security deposit often counts on you giving up. They bank on your frustration.

But tenant rights security deposit laws exist specifically to stop this. In most states, your landlord has a strict deadline to return your money or provide an itemized deduction list. Silence isn't an option for them—it's a violation.

Over the next few days, I'll show you exactly what your landlord can and cannot do, how long they really have to pay you back, and the single most effective way to force their hand before you ever step into a courtroom.

Stay tuned.

The DepositBack Team

P.S. If you want to skip ahead and send a legally sound demand letter today, DepositBack generates one in under 5 minutes."
    },
    {
      "email_number": 2,
      "subject_line": "30 days late? Your landlord is breaking the law",
      "preview_text": "Every state has a deadline. If they've missed it, you're entitled to your full deposit back—sometimes double.",
      "body_copy": "Yesterday we talked about your rights. Today, let's talk about deadlines—because if your security deposit is not returned within your state's required window, your landlord is automatically on the defensive.

Most states give landlords 14 to 60 days to return your deposit. After that? They could owe you the full amount, plus penalties and interest. When a landlord is withholding security deposit funds past the legal limit, they aren't just late—they're breaking the law.

Here's the truth: landlords know most tenants won't track the calendar. They hope you forget.

Don't.

If you're past the deadline, you have immediate leverage. The law is already on your side. Tomorrow, I'll break down exactly what they can legally deduct—because most "cleaning fees" and "repair charges" tenants see are flat-out illegal.

Fight back with facts.

The DepositBack Team"
    },
    {
      "email_number": 3,
      "subject_line": ""Cleaning fees" aren't real deductions (and 4 other lies landlords tell)",
      "preview_text": "Know exactly what a landlord can deduct from your security deposit—and what they can't touch.",
      "body_copy": "Let's get one thing straight: normal wear and tear is not damage.

Scuffed floors from walking. Faded paint from sunlight. Small nail holes from hanging pictures. Under every state's tenant rights security deposit statute, these are NOT valid deductions.

Yet thousands of renters every month ask us, "Can a landlord keep a security deposit for cleaning?" or "What can a landlord deduct from a security deposit legally?" The answer is: far less than they claim.

Landlords can only deduct for actual damage you caused—beyond ordinary use. They cannot deduct for:
• Routine carpet cleaning
• Repainting for normal wear
• Pre-existing issues
• General "make-ready" costs

If your landlord's itemized list is padded with these, they're stealing from you.

Tomorrow, I'll show you the exact letter that makes them pay attention—and fast.

The DepositBack Team"
    },
    {
      "email_number": 4,
      "subject_line": "My landlord won't give me my deposit back—so I sent this",
      "preview_text": "A security deposit dispute letter changes everything. Get a proven template now.",
      "body_copy": "You've learned your rights. You know the deadline. You know their deductions are probably fake.

Now comes the most important step: what to do when a landlord doesn't return your deposit.

Answer: You hit them with a formal security deposit dispute letter.

Not a text. Not an angry email. A proper, state-law-backed demand letter that cites the exact statutes they've violated and sets a hard deadline for repayment.

This one document changes the game. It transforms you from a frustrated tenant into a legal threat. In our experience, the majority of landlords fold immediately when they see a demand letter—because they know they're wrong.

"My landlord won't give me my deposit back" used to mean
